ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 102263004 refers to yam as a substance; used as closest_parent defensible match for raw peeled yam.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw peeled yam is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a medium-sized chunk or slice used in cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Yam, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11601)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for raw peeled yam (F14070017). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for yam, raw (FDC ID 170071) to confirm macro and micronutrient plausibility. HPB values used as primary; USDA used for secondary nutrient gap-filling where HPB does not report (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, vitamins).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; yam entry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 54 (low) for yam (Dioscorea sp.) is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables of GI and GL values (Am J Clin Nutr 2008;88:5–56). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (54 × 27.88) / 100 ≈ 15.1 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (9 mg/100 g) and exceptionally high potassium (816 mg/100 g) supporting DASH-diet targets
hypertension.guidance: Raw peeled yam is an excellent choice for hypertension management due to its very low sodium content of 9 mg/100 g and high potassium of 816 mg/100 g, which together support vasodilation and blood pressure reduction; patients should avoid adding salt during preparation to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (27.9 g/100 g) offset by low GI of 54 and high dietary fibre of 4.1 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yam has a low glycemic index of 54 and provides 4.1 g/100 g dietary fibre, which slows glucose absorption and supports post-prandial glycaemic control; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 100–150 g per meal and avoid sweetened preparations to keep glycaemic load within target.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low total fat (0.17 g/100 g), negligible saturated fat (0.04 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and high potassium and fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw peeled yam is cardioprotective due to its negligible fat and saturated fat content, zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (4.1 g/100 g) which supports LDL reduction, and high potassium (816 mg/100 g) which supports arterial health; it is suitable as a regular starchy staple in a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome modulation and anti-inflammatory pathways; free of pro-inflammatory fats
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw yam must be thoroughly cooked before consumption to neutralise calcium oxalate crystals that can irritate mucous membranes; once cooked, its high fibre and low-fat profile support anti-inflammatory dietary patterns relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yam, raw, peeled (F14070017) is a raw starchy root vegetable belonging to the Dioscorea genus, most commonly Dioscorea alata (greater yam or water yam), with the outer skin removed. The flesh is white to cream-coloured, firm, and slightly waxy in texture, with a mild, earthy, starchy flavour that becomes soft and slightly sweet upon cooking. Raw yam is not typically consumed uncooked due to the presence of calcium oxalate crystals, which can cause oral irritation; it is almost always boiled, steamed, roasted, or incorporated into soups and stews before eating. Per 100 g, raw peeled yam provides 118 kcal, 27.9 g carbohydrate, 1.53 g protein, 0.17 g fat, and 4.1 g dietary fibre, making it a moderate-energy, high-fibre starchy food. Its glycemic index is approximately 54 (low), which is notably lower than white rice or white bread, supporting more stable post-prandial blood glucose responses. Potassium content is exceptionally high at 816 mg/100 g, making it one of the richest plant-based potassium sources and highly suitable for DASH-diet approaches to hypertension management. Sodium is very low at 9 mg/100 g. Cholesterol is absent. Yam is widely consumed across tropical Asia, West Africa, the Caribbean, and the Pacific Islands, and features prominently in Singaporean, Malaysian, and Southeast Asian cuisines as a component of soups, desserts, and savoury dishes. It is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Clinically, raw yam is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes (with portion control), and cardiovascular disease, and its anti-inflammatory fibre profile is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis when the food is properly cooked.
common_user_queries: What is the glycemic index of yam?
Is yam good for blood pressure?
Can I eat yam if I have hypertension?
What is the difference between yam and sweet potato?
meal_context: used in yam cake (or wu tao gao) preparation
incorporated into desserts such as yam paste (orh nee)

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record F14070017 with confidence 0.95. Secondary nutrients cross-referenced and confirmed against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170071) for yam, raw. GI value of 54 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed International Tables of GI and GL (2008). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and GI are fully supported by approved sources.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.04 g), monounsaturated fat (0.01 g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.07 g) values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170071) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; these are used as cross-reference estimates and are consistent with the total fat of 0.17 g reported by HPB. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed as negligible/trace for raw plant foods per USDA data. 3. Vitamin and mineral values (vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170071) as HPB does not report these micronutrients for this entry. 4. Cholesterol is 0.0 g — confirmed zero for this plant-based food per both HPB and USDA sources. 5. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg and vitamin D is 0.0 mcg — confirmed zero/not present in plant-based raw yam per USDA SR Legacy. 6. Raw yam contains calcium oxalate crystals and should always be cooked before consumption. 7. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (54 × 27.88) / 100 = 15.1 for a 100 g serving. 8. This food is a generic raw ingredient record; no Singaporean-dish multilingual naming requirements apply as this is not a prepared Singaporean dish. Local-language names are not applicable for this generic ingredient record and are set to null accordingly. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g: values populated from USDA cross-reference; not reported as -1 as defensible values are available from an approved secondary source.
